coredata.dc:title: Standing wave-assisted acoustic droplet vaporization for single and dual payload release in acoustically-responsive scaffolds
coredata.dc:creator: Aliabouzar, Mitra
Jivani, Aniket
Lu, Xiaofang
Kripfgans, Oliver D.
Fowlkes, J. Brian
Fabiilli, Mario L.
coredata.dc:description: An ultrasound standing wave field (SWF) has been utilized in many biomedical applications. Here, we demonstrate how a SWF can enhance drug release using acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV) in an acoustically-responsive scaffold (ARS). ARSs are composite fibrin hydrogels containing payload-carrying, monodispersed perfluorocarbon (PFC) emulsions and have been used to stimulate regenerative processes such as angiogenesis. Elevated amplitudes in the SWF significantly enhanced payload release from ARSs containing dextran-loaded emulsions (nominal diameter: 6 μm) compared to the -SWF condition, both at sub- and suprathreshold excitation pressures. At 2.5 MHz and 4 MPa peak rarefactional pressure, the cumulative percentage of payload released from ARSs reached 84.1 ± 5.4% and 66.1 ± 4.4% under + SWF and -SWF conditions, respectively, on day 10. A strategy for generating a SWF for an in situ ARS is also presented. For dual-payload release studies, bi-layer ARSs containing a different payload within each layer were exposed to temporally staggered ADV at 3.25 MHz (day 0) and 8.6 MHz (day 4). Sequential payload release was demonstrated using dextran payloads as well as two growth factors relevant to angiogenesis: basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF) and platelet-derived growth factor BB (PDGF-BB). In addition, bubble growth and fibrin degradation were characterized in the ARSs under +SWF and -SWF conditions. These results highlight the utility of a SWF for modulating single and dual payload release from an ARS and can be used in future therapeutic studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Picture of ultrasound (US) exposure setup. The plastic balls at the water surface maintain the temperature and dissolved gas concentration in the water tank. Schematics of side views of (B) a single-layer acoustically-responsive scaffold (ARS) containing perfluorohexane (C6)-dextran488 emulsion with varying volumes of overlying media (i.e., 0, 0.5, and 2 mL), (C) single-layer ARS backed with pre-formed bubbles generated from perfluoropentane (C5) emulsions, and (D) a bi-layer ARS loaded with 0.5% (v/v) C6-dextran488 emulsion in the lower layer (i.e., ARSlower) and 1% (v/v) C6-dextran594 emulsion in the upper layer (i.e., ARSupper) polymerized in a BioFlex plate. The plate was positioned on the surface of a water tank and US was applied through the well bottom. The top view of an ARS, total well diameter (dt), well diameter exposable to US (dUS), and lateral raster spacing (dx) of the US focus are shown in panel E. Scale bar: 20 mm.

(A) Peak-to-peak RF voltage of the scattered response, and (B) corresponding integrated frequency power spectrum from acoustically-responsive scaffolds (ARSs) containing a single-payload (i.e., 0.5% (v/v) C6-dextran488) at 2.5 MHz using a passive detection method. Using piece-wise linear fitting, the acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV) and inertial cavitation (IC) thresholds for the ARS were 2.2 ± 0.2 MPa (n = 5) and 3.9 ± 0.2 MPa (n = 5), respectively, at 2.5 MHz. Similarly, the IC threshold for the fibrin-only gels was 7.8 ± 0.1 MPa (n = 5).

(A) Axial spatial pressure distribution of ultrasound (US) at 2.5 MHz and 2.5 MPa peak rarefactional pressure inside a BioFlex well plate. Zero distance is the position of the air-DMEM interface in the case of an US standing wave field (SWF) and DMEM-DMEM interface in the -SWF (i.e., traveling field) condition (n = 6). (B) Single-payload release profiles from ARSs containing 0.5% (v/v) C6-dextran488 under + SWF (i.e., hom = 0) and -SWF (i.e., hom = 18λ) conditions at 2.5 MHz (Pr = 1.5 MPa) (n = 8).

Release profiles of acoustically-responsive scaffolds (ARSs) containing 0.5% (v/v) C6-dextran488 following a single exposure to ultrasound (US) on day 0 at (A) 4 MPa (n = 8) and (B) 8 MPa (n = 8) peak rarefactional pressure (Pr) with varying overlying media heights (hom). (C) Rate constants (K) were determined by fitting the release profiles in panels (A) and (B) to a first order exponential model. (D) Representative degradation of ARSs containing fibrinogen647 following ADV at 2.5 MHz (Pr = 4 and 8 MPa) in the presence (hom = 0) and absence (hom = 18λ) of an ultrasound standing wave field (n = 8). Statistically significant differences (p < 0.05) are denoted as follows: α: vs. Pr = 8 MPa, hom = 0; β: vs. 8 MPa, hom = 18λ; γ: vs. 4 MPa, hom = 0.

Images of acoustically-responsive-scaffolds (ARSs) exposed to ultrasound (US) in the (A) presence and absence of an US standing wave field (SWF) on day 0. Panel (B) is a magnified area shown in panel (A), denoting the interface between the two exposure conditions. Panels (C) and (D) display the ARSs on day 10 in -SWF and + SWF conditions, respectively. US exposure was carried out at 2.5 MHz and 4 MPa peak rarefactional pressure. Scale bar: 0.5 mm.

Release profiles from acoustically responsive scaffolds (ARSs) exposed to ultrasound (US) at 2.5 MHz and 4 MPa peak rarefactional pressure under three conditions: +SWF (hom = 0), -SWF (hom = 18λ), and -SWF with a pre-formed C5 bubble wall on the top (n = 8).

Spatially-controlled release from bi-layer acoustically-responsive scaffolds (ARSs), containing two dextran payloads. Release profiles (A) and the corresponding macroscopic image (B) of ARSs when only the upper layer was exposed to an ultrasound (US) standing wave field at 3.25 MHz and 3 MPa peak rarefactional pressure (Pr) on day 0 (n = 8). Release profiles (C) and the corresponding macroscopic image (D) of ARSs when only the lower layer was exposed to US at 8.6 MHz (Pr = 7 MPa) on day 0 (n = 8). Scale bar: 1 mm.

Frequency-dependent sequential release from bi-layer acoustically-responsive scaffolds (ARSs) doped with (A) dual dextran-loaded (n = 8), and (B) dual growth factor-loaded (n = 4) perfluorohexane (C6) emulsions. The first payload was released by ultrasound (US) on day 0. The arrow shows the US activation of the second payload on day 4.
Ultrasound (US) parameters, including frequency of excitation (f), peak rarefactional pressure (Pr), raster spacing (dx), and emulsion formulations used for single- and dual-payload release studies.

head.ce:title: Standing wave-assisted acoustic droplet vaporization for single and dual payload release in acoustically-responsive scaffolds

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ultrasound standing wave field (SWF) has been utilized in many biomedical applications. Here, we demonstrate how a SWF can enhance drug release using acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV) in an acoustically-responsive scaffold (ARS). ARSs are composite fibrin hydrogels containing payload-carrying, monodispersed perfluorocarbon (PFC) emulsions and have been used to stimulate regenerative processes such as angiogenesis. Elevated amplitudes in the SWF significantly enhanced payload release from ARSs containing dextran-loaded emulsions (nominal diameter: 6 μm) compared to the -SWF condition, both at sub- and suprathreshold excitation pressures. At 2.5 MHz and 4 MPa peak rarefactional pressure, the cumulative percentage of payload released from ARSs reached 84.1 ± 5.4% and 66.1 ± 4.4% under + SWF and -SWF conditions, respectively, on day 10. A strategy for generating a SWF for an in situ ARS is also presented. For dual-payload release studies, bi-layer ARSs containing a different payload within each layer were exposed to temporally staggered ADV at 3.25 MHz (day 0) and 8.6 MHz (day 4). Sequential payload release was demonstrated using dextran payloads as well as two growth factors relevant to angiogenesis: basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF) and platelet-derived growth factor BB (PDGF-BB). In addition, bubble growth and fibrin degradation were characterized in the ARSs under +SWF and -SWF conditions. These results highlight the utility of a SWF for modulating single and dual payload release from an ARS and can be used in future therapeutic studies.

ce:section.ce:para: Conventional hydrogel-based, drug delivery approaches are mainly governed by passive mechanisms like diffusion and degradation. Therefore, after implantation of a hydrogel scaffold, the release kinetics of deliverable therapeutic agents (e.g., growth factors) cannot be altered. Designing scaffold-based delivery systems that enable active modulation of release, via an externally-controlled mechanism, would be beneficial. Ultrasound (US)-triggered drug delivery can induce activation with temporal and spatial specificity, thereby providing non-invasive and on-demand control of release kinetics. One application where spatiotemporally-controlled release is critically important is tissue regeneration, since biological processes are regulated by the spatial profile and temporal expression of molecules like growth factors [1].

We have utilized acoustic droplet vaporization (ADV), a threshold-based phenomenon, to control release of therapeutic agents encapsulated in phase shift emulsions with a double emulsion structure of water-in-perfluorocarbon (PFC)-in-water (W1/PFC/W2) [2,3]. The deliverable payload is contained within the innermost water phase (i.e., W1). US can disrupt the morphology of the double emulsion by phase-transitioning the PFC phase, thereby releasing the payload. Phase shift emulsions can be incorporated into implantable fibrin scaffolds to produce a composite hydrogel, termed an acoustically-responsive scaffold (ARS). ARSs have be used in conjunction with ADV in both in vitro [4] and in vivo [5] studies to control delivery of fluorescently-labeled dextran as well as basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF). ADV-triggered delivery of bFGF stimulated endothelial network formation in vitro [6] and subcutaneous angiogenesis in vivo [7]. Given the necessity for multiple growth factors in tissue regeneration, ARSs have also been used for the sequential delivery of two surrogate payloads by encapsulating each payload in separate emulsions, each having a unique ADV threshold [8].

To optimize release of a therapeutic agent from an ARS, two criteria are important. First, the emulsion within the ARS should be thermally stable at body temperature, therefore only releasing its payload when exposed to suprathreshold US. Second, when exposed to suprathreshold US, the fraction of droplets that undergo ADV (i.e., the ADV efficiency) should be high. PFCs with higher bulk boiling points offer better thermal stability by eliminating any potential spontaneous vaporization as well as higher probability of repeated vaporization and re-condensation which are desired for extended diagnostic and therapeutic applications [9]. However, the ADV threshold correlates with the boiling point of the PFC in the emulsion. Thus, in order to achieve a high ADV efficiency using higher boiling point PFCs, greater acoustic input energies are required, which can limit their use in biomedical applications. Alternatively, creating an US standing wave field (SWF) can generate elevated amplitudes due to constructive superposition of a reflected wave at the interface of two media of differing acoustic impedances with an incident wave. A SWF has been used in biomedical applications including sonoporation-induced intracellular drug delivery [10], US-mediated gene delivery [11], cell banding [12,13], and particle sorting [14]. Lo, Kripfgans [15] showed that creating a plane of pre-formed bubbles further enhanced ADV on the proximal side of the bubble wall.

In this work, we created a SWF to generate a suprathreshold pressure field at subthreshold excitation pressures. The acoustic pressure field was characterized under +SWF and –SWF (i.e., without establishment of standing waves in the US field) conditions. The effect of a SWF on the release profile of single-layer ARSs doped with dextran-containing monodispersed emulsions was investigated at varying peak rarefactional pressures. A strategy for generating a SWF for an ARS implanted in situ was also experimentally verified. Sequential release of two payloads was conducted by integrating the SWF with the frequency-dependence of the ADV threshold in a bi-layer ARS containing two dextran payloads. Similarly, we also demonstrated for the first time, sequential release of two growth factors relevant to angiogenesis, bFGF and platelet-derived growth factor BB (PDGF-BB), from bi-layer ARSs. bFGF is involved with the sprouting of new blood vessels while PDGF-BB stimulates neovessel stabilization via the recruitment of pericytes [16,17]. bFGF and PDGF-BB are mutually antagonistic when delivered simultaneously [18,19]. In addition, growth of the ADV-generated bubbles in ARSs was compared optically in the presence and absence of a SWF. Finally, the combined effect of ADV and subsequent bubble growth on the fibrin network degradation in ARSs was investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of monodisperse double emulsions
ce:section.ce:para: Micron-sized, double emulsions with a W1/PFC/W2 structure were prepared using a microfluidic-based technique following a previously described method [7]. Perfluoropentane (C5, CAS# 678-26-2, bulk boiling point: 29 °C, Strem Chemicals, Newburyport, MA, USA), perfluorohexane (C6, CAS# 355-42-0, bulk boiling point: 56 °C, Strem Chemicals), or perfluorononane (C9, CAS# 375-96-2, bulk boiling point: 125 °C, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) was used as the PFC phase. A fluorosurfactant copolymer, synthesized using a 2:1 M ratio of Krytox 157 FSH (CAS# 51798-33-5, DuPont, Wilmington, DE, USA) and poly(ethylene glycol) bis(amine) (MW: 1000 g/mol, CAS# 24991-53-5, Alfa Aesar, Ward Hill, MA, USA), was dissolved at 2% (w/w) in PFC. The PFC solution was combined at 2:1 (v/v) with a W1 phase containing either 1.66 mg/mL Alexa Fluor 488-labeled dextran (dextran488, MW: 10,000 Da, Life Technologies, Grand Island, NY, USA) or Alexa Fluor 594-labeled dextran (dextran594, MW: 10,000 Da, Life Technologies) in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, Life Technologies), and then sonicated (Q55 with CL-188 immersion probe, QSonica, LLC, Newton, CT, USA) for 30 s while on ice. For blank emulsions, the W1 phase contained only PBS. For emulsions containing bFGF or PDGF-BB, the W1 phase contained 10 mg/mL bovine serum albumin (Sigma-Aldrich) and 0.4 U/mL heparin (Calbiochem, San Diego, CA, USA) in PBS and either 0.5 mg/mL human recombinant bFGF (Cat#: GF003AF, EMD Millipore, Temecula, CA, USA) or 25 μg/mL human recombinant PDGF-BB (Cat#: 220-BB, R&D Systems, Minneapolis, MN, USA), respectively.

To produce the double emulsion, the primary emulsion and W2 phase, which was 50 mg/mL Pluronic F68 (CAS# 9003-11-6, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) in PBS, were pumped at 1 µL/min and 10 µL/min, respectively, through a quartz microfluidic chip (Cat# 3200146, junction: 14 µm
×
17 µm, Dolomite, Royston, United Kingdom). The size distribution, coefficient of variation, and concentration of the double emulsion were analyzed using a Coulter Counter (Multisizer 4, Beckman Coulter, Brea, CA, USA) with a 50 µm aperture tube. The encapsulation efficiency of each payload in the double emulsions was measured, as done previously, by breaking the emulsion using a probe sonicator (Branson 450A, Emerson, St. Louis, MO, USA) [20]. In this work, each emulsion is designated by its respective PFC and payload (e.g., C6-dextran488).

ARSs were prepared by first dissolving bovine fibrinogen (Sigma-Aldrich) in FluoroBrite Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM, Life Technologies) at 20 mg/mL clottable protein while under gentle vortex mixing for 30 s. The fibrinogen solution and additional DMEM were degassed in a vacuum chamber (Isotemp vacuum oven, Model 282A, Fisher Scientific, Dubuque, IA, USA) at ~6 kPa for 60 min to minimize the amount of dissolved gas, which could act as cavitation nuclei. ARSs were made by combining the prepared fibrinogen, DMEM, 0.5% or 1% (v/v) double emulsion, bovine aprotinin (Sigma-Aldrich), and bovine thrombin (Thrombin-JMI, King Pharmaceuticals, Bristol, TN, USA) under sterile conditions. The final concentration of fibrinogen, aprotinin, and bovine thrombin in the ARSs were 10 mg/mL, 0.05 U/mL, and 2 U/mL, respectively. ARSs were cast in 24-well BioFlex plates (well diameter: 15 mm, well diameter exposable to US: 13.5 mm, membrane thickness: 0.2 mm, and total height: 15 mm, Flexcell International, Burlington, NC, USA), as shown in Fig. 1 , and allowed to polymerize for 15 min at room temperature.

For single-payload release studies, 0.5 mL aliquots (height: ~3 mm) of the ARS mixture containing 0.5% (v/v) C6-dextran488 were added into each well. For dual-payload release studies bi-layer ARSs, each having a nominal height of approximately 2 mm, were prepared as follows. The bottom layer (i.e., adjacent to the well bottom), containing 0.5% (v/v) C6-dextran488 or C6-PDGF-BB, was polymerized first. Then, the top layer, loaded with 1% (v/v) C6-dextran594 or C6-bFGF, was aliquoted on top of the bottom layer and allowed to polymerize. Previously, we showed that reflectivity of ARSs increased linearly with the volume fraction of emulsion as well as the excitation frequency [21]. Therefore, in this study, a lower volume fraction of C6 emulsion was used in the bottom layer. After US exposure, ARSs were covered with 0.5 mL overlying media consisting of DMEM supplemented with 100 U/mL penicillin, 100 µg/mL streptomycin, and 2.5 µg/mL amphotericin B (Life Technologies). The morphology of single and bi-layer ARSs was imaged using a stereoscope (MZ FLIII, Leica, Deerfield, IL, USA).

All experiments were conducted in a water tank (30 cm × 60 cm × 30 cm) filled with degassed (12–22% O2 saturation, where 100% O2 means equilibrium with air), deionized water at 37 °C, as shown in Fig. 1(A). A calibrated, focused transducer (H-108, f-number = 0.83, radius of curvature = 50 mm, Sonic Concepts Inc., Bothell, WA, USA) was driven at either fundamental (2.5 or 3.25 MHz) or 3rd harmonic (8.6 MHz) mode to generate ADV within the ARSs. These frequencies were verified by the manufacturer to produce optimal outputs at the nominal fundamental and third harmonic frequencies.

Pulsed waveforms (pulse duration: 5.4 µs; pulse repetition frequency (PRF): 100 Hz; duty cycle: 0.05%) were generated by a function generator (33500B, Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and amplified by a gated radiofrequency (RF) amplifier (68 dB gain for the fundamental or 60 dB gain for the 3rd harmonic modes, GA-2500A Ritec Inc., Warwick, RI, USA). The generated amplified signals were viewed and monitored in real-time on an oscilloscope (HDO4034, Teledyne LeCroy, Chestnut Ridge, NY, USA). To reduce the impedance mismatch between the transducer and amplifier, matching circuits (H108_3 MN, Sonic Concepts) were used. The transducer was calibrated in free field at the focus in the range of 1–8 MPa and 1–7 MPa peak rarefactional pressure at the fundamental and 3rd harmonic, respectively, using an in-house fiber optic hydrophone (sensitivity: 16.6 mV/MPa) with a fiber diameter of 105 µm [22]. Unless otherwise noted, all acoustic pressures are listed as peak rarefactional pressures (Pr).

Experiments were conducted by placing the BioFlex plate containing the ARSs in the water tank such that only the bottom of the plate was in contact with water (Fig. 1(A)). The transducer was positioned under the plate and connected to a three-axis positioning system controlled by MATLAB (The MathWorks, Natick, MA, USA). The transducer was continuously rastered at 5 mm/s for all the release studies. To localize the transducer axially with respect to the ARSs, a pulse echo technique was utilized whereby the transducer was driven by a pulser-receiver (5077PR, Olympus, Center Valley, PA, US) at low pressures to avoid ADV. The axial focus of the transducer was positioned and maximized with respect to reflections from the well bottom (silicone elastomer membrane with a characteristic impedance (Z) of ~1.2 MRayl) as well as the overlying media-air interface (Z ~ 0.43 MRayl) [23]. Single-payload release studies were performed at 2.5 MHz while dual-payload release experiments were carried out at 3.25 MHz and 8.6 MHz. The complete description of single- and dual-payload release studies is given in section 2.6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ADV and inertial cavitation (IC) threshold measurements in ARSs
ce:section.ce:para: The ADV and IC thresholds were measured by exposing ARSs containing 0.5% (v/v) C6-dextran488 to increasing acoustic pressure from the transducer, following previously described methods [7,24]. Briefly, a calibrated hydrophone (HGL- 0085, frequency range: 1–50 MHz, Onda, Sunnyvale, CA USA), which was placed 5 cm away from the focus of the transducer, passively detected backscattered acoustic signals generated in the ARSs during US exposure. The acquired RF signals were digitized by the oscilloscope (sampling rate = 100 MHz) and analyzed in MATLAB. To determine the ADV threshold, the peak-to-peak amplitude of the scattered response, which increased significantly following ADV, was plotted as a function of Pr. To compute the IC threshold, the integrated frequency power spectrum of the backscattered signals was analyzed according to previously described methods [7].

The acoustic pressure distribution in the presence and absence of a SWF was characterized in the 24-well BioFlex plate while in the water tank. The fiber optic hydrophone, connected to a three-axis positioning system, and the digital oscilloscope were used to measure the acoustic pressure field. The well was filled with 0.5 mL DMEM, which approximated the height of the ARSs, and the hydrophone tip was placed at the air-DMEM interface. The air interface above the well was used as an acoustic reflector to generate a SWF within the samples. Measurements were recorded from the interface at 50 µm intervals. The axial focus of the transducer, driven at its fundamental frequency of 2.5 MHz (Pr = 2.5 MPa), was placed at mid-height in the DMEM. To compare the axial distribution of pressure in a SWF to a travelling field (-SWF), the air was replaced with DMEM by completely filling the well without moving the position of the hydrophone. Interference patterns were plotted in terms of absolute Pr. Note that the measured US attenuation of the silicone elastomer well bottom (thickness ~ 0.2 mm) of the BioFlex plates was calculated to be 0.7 ± 0.2 dB/MHz/cm [13], indicating insignificant interference with the sound field at this frequency.

To investigate the effect of a SWF on single-payload release, ARSs containing 0.5% (v/v) C6-dextran488 were covered with varying volumes of overlying media (i.e., 0, 0.5, and 2 mL) during US exposure. The corresponding overlying media height (hom), expressed in terms of wavelength (λ), was 0, 4.6λ, and 18λ, respectively, where hom = 0 was considered a SWF condition. In another case, each well was completely filled with overlying media, sealed by a Tegaderm membrane (3 M Health Care, St. Paul, MN, USA), and submerged in the water tank. US exposure parameters for payload release studies are listed in Table 1 . For all single-payload release studies, the axial focus of the transducer was positioned at mid-height of each ARS. However, the lateral spacing (dx) was chosen according to the full width half maximum (FWHM) of the beam width at the focus [25] and was calculated based on the f-number of the transducer and the wavelength at the given excitation frequency (Table 1).

Immediately after US exposure, the overlying media was completely removed and replaced with 0.5 mL fresh media. ARSs were then placed in a standard tissue culture incubator (37 °C, 5% carbon dioxide) throughout the duration of the experiments. Overlying media was sampled daily, including three hours post exposure, by collecting half of the media and replacing it with an equal volume of fresh media until 10 days after exposure.

Bi-layer ARSs were generated for dual-payload release studies with details listed in Table 1. For the first payload release, the focus of the transducer was placed at the ARS-air interface. Following US exposure, ARSs were covered with 0.5 mL overlying media and placed in the incubator. For the second payload release, the focus of the transducer was positioned 0.5 mm into the ARS (i.e., 0.5 mm above the silicone membrane). For dual-payload release studies, overlying media was sampled for 14 days after the first exposure. Similarly, daily samples were taken from ARSs exposed to sham US as controls for both single- and dual- payload release studies.

For studies to simulate SWF conditions for translatable applications, a bi-layer ARS was fabricated such that the upper layer (height ~ 2 mm) contained a pre-formed bubble wall by using C5 emulsion as described in Table 1 and shown schematically in Fig. 1(C). C5 is a volatile PFC liquid with a bulk boiling point of 29 °C. After polymerization of these bi-layer gels, the ARSs were placed in the incubator overnight to allow the C5 emulsion to vaporize due to thermal instability. Once the bubble wall (i.e., vaporized C5 emulsion) was formed (<24 h in the incubator), a similar US exposure was performed.

The concentration of dextran in the collected media was measured using a fluorometer (Molecular Devices Spectramax M2e, Sunnyvale, CA, USA). The concentration of growth factors released from the ARSs was measured using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kits for bFGF (DY233, R&D Systems) and PDGF-BB (DY220, R&D Systems). All calculations were made using a mass balance approach for the payload (e.g., dextran, bFGF, etc.), which accounted for mass removed (i.e., mass removed when sampling the media) and subsequent dilution of the overlying due to addition of additional media [20]. Cumulative payload release was normalized by the encapsulation efficiency of the payload (i.e., dextran and growth factors) within emulsions as well as the exposed area of the ARS to US within the plates (see Fig. 1). Additionally, unlike dextran, bFGF and PDGF-BB display high affinities for fibrin and plastic, respectively [26,27]. For growth factor release studies, data were further normalized by the maximum percent release of unencapsulated bFGF and PDGF from fibrin gels (Supplemental Fig. 1). For the affinity of PDGF for plastic, a solution of the growth factor was added to the BioFlex plate and the concentration was longitudinally measured (Supplemental Fig. 1). However, since the binding affinity for plastic would affect both the unencapsulated PDGF as well as ARSs with emulsions containing PDGF, normalizing the release from ARSs by the maximum percent release from the unencapsulated PDGF was sufficient.

The combined effects of ADV, IC, and subsequent post-excitation bubble growth on degradation of the fibrin network was quantified by adding 39 µg/mL Alexa Fluor 647-labeled fibrinogen (fibrinogen647) to each ARS prior to polymerization. Similar to payload release studies, overlying media was sampled daily until 10 days after US exposure. Cumulative percent of fibrinogen647 released was normalized by the initial amount of fibrinogen647 in the ARSs as well as the exposed area.

Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism software (GraphPad Software, Inc., La Jolla, CA, USA). All data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation. The number of independent replicates is listed in the caption for each figure. ADV and IC data were fit with a piece-wise linear function with 2 pieces. The thresholds were defined as the Pr corresponding to the intersection of the two linear fits. To determine a rate constant (K) for dextran diffusion, the data were fit to a first-order exponential approximation of solute diffusion in a highly porous hydrogel following previously described methods [28,29]. Significant differences between groups were determined using a one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s multiple comparisons test, with a significance level of 0.05.

Sizing characteristics of the emulsions used in the ARSs are summarized in Table 2 . There were no significant differences in mean diameter among the various PFC emulsions. C5-blank emulsions had a broader size distribution as well as coefficient of variation. Overall, the type of payload did not affect the size distribution of the double emulsions used in the ARSs.

The ADV and IC thresholds of ARSs were determined through passive detection of the scattered response and the broadband acoustic signal, respectively. The peak-to-peak RF voltage and the integrated frequency power spectrum from ARSs containing a single payload at an excitation frequency of 2.5 MHz are shown in Fig. 2 (A) and (B), respectively. Using piece-wise linear fitting, the ADV and IC thresholds were 2.2 ± 0.2 MPa and 3.9 ± 0.2 MPa, respectively, at 2.5 MHz. Similarly, the ADV threshold was 5.2 ± 0.3 MPa at 8.6 MHz (data not shown). No IC threshold was detected in ARSs at an excitation frequency of 8.6 MHz across the range of peak rarefactional pressures interrogated here (i.e., 1–7 MPa). Note that bubbles in a confining viscoelastic medium have a higher threshold for IC [30]. The ADV and IC thresholds at 3.25 MHz were not significantly different than 2.5 MHz (data not shown). Consistent with our past studies, ADV occurred at lower rarefactional pressures than IC, indicating that phase transition precedes IC [24,31]. Previously, we showed that the amplitudes of high frequency harmonics were diminished significantly in ARSs containing high volume fractions of C6 emulsion, likely due to the high reflectivity of PFC emulsions, thus causing an increasing trend of ADV threshold with excitation frequency [21]. In addition, the degree of acoustic nonlinearity at the focus significantly decreases for lower f-number transducers [32] since nonlinear effects accumulate with propagation distance [33]. As the peak rarefactional pressure increased, the volume of the beam that was suprathreshold increased, resulting in a higher ADV efficiency and stronger backscattered signal (Fig. 2(A)). The increased backscatter signal at higher excitation pressures might also be reflective of the growth of the ADV-generated bubbles in the focal volume over the duration of the pulse. Parameters such as volume fraction of emulsions in ARSs would affect the ADV efficiency at higher excitation pressures due to the acoustic shielding of the incident pulse by the generated ADV bubbles [21,34].

Additionally, control experiments without emulsions (i.e., fibrin-only gels) showed cavitation activities at peak rarefactional pressures above 7.8 MPa at 2.5 MHz (Fig. 2). To test whether the emulsions could act as cavitation nuclei and reduce the IC threshold in fibrin gels, we ran additional control experiments with PFC emulsions of higher boiling point, C9 (bulk boiling point of 125 °C). The backscattered signal received from C9 emulsions remained comparable to the fibrin-only gel at the ADV threshold recorded for C6 emulsions, indicating that vaporized C6 emulsions were the primary source of increased backscattered signal (data not shown).

The axial pressure distribution of US propagating through a 24-well BioFlex plate in the presence and absence of a SWF are shown in Fig. 3 (A). A distance of zero represents the air-DMEM interface for the case of +SWF, and DMEM-DMEM interface for the -SWF condition. Measurements were recorded 25 µm below the air-DMEM interface due to surface tension effects. The pressure reflection coefficient at a free surface (e.g., the DMEM-air interface) is −1, resulting in total reflection of energy. The interference patterns (i.e., pressure nodes and antinodes), caused by the superposition of incoming and reflected waves, were well-developed in the SWF. For a condition with a rigid boundary at one end (i.e., zero particle velocity) and a pressure release boundary (i.e., zero pressure) at the other end, pressure antinodes develop at (2 m-1) λ/4 and pressure nodes develop at mλ/2, where m is a positive integer [35,36].

The measured distance between pressure antinodes was ~0.3 mm, which was consistent with the half-wavelength spacing between the antinodes for a 2.5 MHz wave in water (λ = 0.6 mm). The amplitude of the developed antinodes in the SWF (i.e., the standing wave ratio) varied from 1.2 to 2.4-fold higher compared to the -SWF condition. The non-zero amplitude of the pressure nodes may be due to the presence and the resulting reflection from the plate walls, making it different from a free field measurement. Pressure doubling due to the SWF has been studied by several groups at low excitation pressures (e.g., 0.04 to 0.1 MPa) through hydrophone measurements and simulations [13,36,37]. In these conditions, nonlinearity due to nonlinear propagation of the wave was minimal (e.g., shock parameter (σ) < 1). However, in our study, σ was greater than 1, which is closer to the waveforms generated by most diagnostic and therapeutic US systems. Depending on σ, the closest pressure antinode would develop at a distance λ/4 (for a linear waveform) to λ/2 (for a sawtooth waveform) from the air interface [38].

Theoretically, the maximum region of interference in the SWF is nλ/2, where n is the number of cycles. Parameters such as pulse length (i.e., number of cycles), f-number of the transducer, position of the focus with respect to the reflecting interface, as well as the wait times between the pulses significantly impact the formation of standing waves. O'Reilly, Huang [39] reported a more pronounced standing wave effect for a loosely focused (f-number = 1.5) versus tightly focused (f-number = 0.8) transducer. With the tighter focus, standing waves were only apparent close to the reflecting interface. In addition, reducing the duty cycle (i.e., longer off-time intervals between the pulses) resulted in a less effective standing wave field [11,39].

Due to the enhanced pressure in the SWF as a result of interference patterns, ADV was achieved at excitation pressures below the ADV threshold. Fig. 3(B) displays the release profiles from single-payload containing ARSs exposed to US in the presence (hom = 0) and absence (hom = 18λ) of a SWF at a subthreshold Pr of 1.5 MPa. Note that the ADV threshold of these ARSs, as shown in Fig. 2(A), was 2.2 ± 0.2 MPa at 2.5 MHz. Following exposure to a subthreshold pressure while in a SWF, 42.4 ± 4.8% of the payload was released from the ARS by day 10 whereas in the -SWF condition, the release profile was comparable to the control (-US).

The effect of overlying media height on release from single-payload containing ARSs was investigated at 2.5 MHz (Pr = 4 MPa). As demonstrated in Fig. 4 (A), suprathreshold exposure with a SWF generated significantly greater payload release from the ARS than in the -SWF condition. No statistically significant differences in payload release were observed at any time point between conditions with hom of 4.6λ or 18λ as well as the submerged condition. The cumulative percentage of payload released from ARSs under + SWF reached 67.0 ± 4.4% (on day 5) and 84.1 ± 5.4% (on day 10), which was 45% and 21% higher than the same time points with overlying media, respectively. The narrowing differential in payload release over time may be due to the presence of dense, stable bubbles generated by ADV under the SWF, resulting in a longer diffusion path length and delaying the further release of dextran488. Lu, Dong [29] showed that the rate constant for payload release decreased in the presence of stable bubbles. The diffusion coefficient of tracer molecules (typically in the range of a few µm2/s) in gels correlated indirectly with gel density as well as presence of gas bubbles and cells due to the tortuous path encountered by the diffusing molecules [40,41].

Similar experiments were conducted at a peak rarefactional pressure of 8 MPa which was significantly above the ADV and IC thresholds in ARSs at 2.5 MHz. As demonstrated in Fig. 4(B), ARSs exposed to the SWF displayed greater release than the -SWF condition over the first three days. However, payload release for both conditions was not significantly different beginning on day 4. At 8 MPa peak rarefactional pressure, the focal volume was already suprathreshold in the -SWF, thus pressure amplification generated in the +SWF would not impact the ADV efficiency.

Release profiles from ARSs in Fig. 4(A) and (B) were fit to a first-order exponential model to approximate a rate constant (K) for dextran diffusion and are shown in Fig. 4(C). The coefficient of determination (R2) ranged from 0.88 to 0.98 for the data. ARSs exposed to higher excitation pressures displayed significantly greater values of K. Fig. 4(C) demonstrates that release kinetics can be tuned for different applications, where slower or faster release is desired, by modulating the excitation amplitude.

In this study, a single z-plane of US exposure was used for payload release. Beam characterization of the transducer at the focus, using the hydrophone, yielded an axial length and lateral width at FWHM of 3.9 ± 0.1 mm (theoretical value: 3.28 mm) and 0.7 ± 0.1 mm (theoretical value: 0.51 mm), respectively, at 2.5 MHz. Since the height of the ARSs was ~3 mm, a single plane of exposure was deemed sufficient. Previously, we showed that payload release from ARSs with perfluoroheptane (C7) or perfluorooctane (C8) was more dependent on the number of z-planes than with C5 or C6, possibly due to re-condensation of higher boiling point PFCs [29].

Fibrin is often used to make implantable hydrogels due to its biocompatibility and propensity to direct tissue regeneration. Representative degradation of the fibrin network in ARSs, indicated longitudinally by release of fibrinogen647, is displayed in Fig. 4(D). On day 10 post-ADV, fibrin degradation was significantly lower (up to 43%) for all ARSs with ADV compared to the control (-US) and correlated inversely with excitation pressure. The decreasing correlation was statistically verified. For the non-control conditions, fibrin degradation was significantly different (by 20%) between 4 MPa (–SWF) and 8 MPa (+SWF) as well as 4 MPa (–SWF) and 8 MPa (–SWF) conditions, on day 10. Fibrin degradation reached 64.2 ± 5.6%, 46.3 ± 6.1%, and 38.5 ± 4.0% for the -US, 4 MPa (-SWF), and 8 MPa (+SWF) conditions on day 10, respectively. Degradation profiles of ARSs in Fig. 4 (D) were fit to a first-order exponential model to approximate the degradation rate (data not shown). Fibrin degradation rate (i.e., K) was significantly different between the –US (0.16 ± 0.04 day−1) and 8 MPa (+SWF) (0.07 ± 0.03 day−1) as well as –US and 8 MPa (-SWF) (0.10 ± 0.03 day−1) conditions. Mechanical strain can slow the rate of fibrin degradation [42–44]. Physical parameters such as pore size, fiber density, and fibrin stiffness have been reported to impact fibrin degradation rate [45,46]. Varjú, Sótonyi [45] showed that mechanical strain significantly decreased the fiber diameter and the average pore size within the fibrin network, as a result of fiber alignment, impeding diffusivity in the direction perpendicular to the strain axis. In our study, both the initial size of the emulsions (diameter: ~ 6 µm) and the ADV-generated bubbles were much larger than the fibrin network pores [47]. As such, they were in direct contact with the fibers as shown in our previous studies [4,6]. The volumetric expansion of the emulsions during ADV, successive expansion and contraction cycles post-ADV, and further growth of the generated bubbles due to in-gassing over the time points studied here induced repetitive localized strain in the fibrin gel. Radial diffusivity in fibrin was shown to decrease exponentially with strain [46]. Note that the oscillation amplitude of pulsating bubbles correlated directly with the excitation amplitude [47]. For ARSs with an IC threshold of 3.9 ± 0.2 MPa at 2.5 MHz, a significantly higher amount of strain was applied to the fibrin boundaries at an excitation amplitude of 8 MPa compared to 4 MPa. Additionally, the diffusivity of degraded fibrinogen647 could be slower due to the presence of macroscopically stable bubbles, which would increase the effective diffusion path length. A qualitative comparison of bubble size and density generated via ADV, in the presence and absence of a SWF, is described in section 3.6.

In addition, mechanical strain results in a densification of the fibrin network [48] and a higher total elastic modulus [49], indicating a strain stiffening behavior [50]. Several studies have shown a faster diffusion of tissue plasminogen activator through a loose network of fibrin fibers than a dense one, indicating an inverse correlation between fibrin stiffness and susceptibility to degradation [51–53]. Previously, we showed that ADV caused consolidation of the fibrin at the margins of generated bubbles and a significant increase in shear stiffness in ARSs using confocal fluorescence microscopy and dynamic shear testing, respectively [4].

Understanding the mechanism of fibrin degradation is crucial since it impacts both controlled and uncontrolled release of payloads in ARSs as well as the rate of cellular infiltration and regenerative processes in the scaffold. In addition, changes in degradation of ARSs, either as a result of hindered diffusivity or increased stiffness due to mechanical strain, can also affect the oxygen and nutrient permeability as well as transport of metabolites which can significantly influence cellular processes. Previous studies showed that slight changes in the mechanical properties of hydrogels, such as stiffness and permeability, significantly altered the proliferation and invasion rate of fibroblasts [54,55]. Here, we studied the bulk degradation of fibrin in ARSs as a result of ADV, IC, and subsequent bubble growth on a daily time scale. Further studies, using high speed cameras and time-lapsed confocal microscopy are required, to fully and independently investigate the effect of each phenomenon on the porous viscoelastic fibrin network on a microscale level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic morphologies of ARSs in the presence and absence of a SWF
ce:section.ce:para: The microscopic morphologies of the ARSs exposed to US at 2.5 MHz (Pr = 4 MPa) under +SWF and -SWF conditions are displayed in Fig. 5 . ADV-generated bubbles were visible on the surface of ARSs in the +SWF on the day of exposure (Fig. 5 A and B) as well as 10 days post-exposure (Fig. 5 D). No bubbles were present on the ARS surface on the day of exposure (Fig. 5 B) in the -SWF condition. The bubble density generated via ADV with the SWF was higher than the -SWF case, as shown in Fig. 5 (A) & (B), as well as the single-payload release results. Therefore, the growth rate of bubbles generated by the SWF would be smaller compared to the –SWF condition as the generated bubbles would compete for the same dissolved gas in the surrounding media. As seen in Fig. 5 (C) & (D), larger bubbles were visible in the -SWF case on day 10. This is also consistent with previous studies predicting a larger growth rate for bubbles in a dilute regime compared to a dense regime [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generating a SWF condition for in situ applications
ce:section.ce:para: Although standing waves can be beneficial for biomedical applications [10,11], reliably producing this condition for translatable applications is rarely achievable, except for structures surrounding gas-filled organs, skull, and bone. In order to implement a more realizable SWF for in vivo translation, bi-layer scaffolds were fabricated where the bottom layer consisted of a single-payload containing ARSs and the top layer contained an ARS with pre-formed C5 bubbles (Table 1), to mimic the air interface as described in section 2.6. We repeated the experiments for three conditions of ARSs in the presence and absence of a SWF and ARSs having pre-formed bubbles from C5 emulsion on the top at 2.5 MHz (Pr = 4 MPa). Previously, we showed that the rate of payload release was lower in ARSs with lower boiling point PFC emulsions such as C5 and C6, compared to higher boiling point PFCs, possibly due to stable bubble formation post ADV [29]. In order to account for the potential decrease in the rate of payload release stemming from the presence of stable bubbles generated from C5 emulsions, a similar 2 mm-layer of ARS containing C5 emulsion was cast on the ARSs in the other two conditions immediately after US exposure. As seen in Fig. 6 , payload release from ARSs with a pre-formed C5 bubble wall during US exposure was not significantly different than the +SFW condition. Both were significantly higher (i.e., up to 30% by day 10) than the -SWF condition. Fig. 6 suggests that backscatter from the bubble wall, even if incoherent, could enhance the pressure field due to constructive interference, resulting in a higher ADV efficiency. When using a bubble wall as a perfect reflector, both the size of the bubbles and the thickness of the wall should be considered in order to have a more effective backscattered energy. Lo, Kripfgans [15] showed that forming a pre-existing bubble wall using ADV with smaller C5 droplets (5 µm in diameter vs 16 µm) increased the effectiveness of the bubble wall due to a higher and more uniform bubble density. Using microbubbles as a tool to reduce the input energy required for therapeutic applications of US such as hyperthermia has been reported before [57].

Sequential release from bi-layer ARSs containing two dextran payloads was performed using two different excitation frequencies. In order to ensure the spatial selectivity of release, the bi-layer ARS was exposed to each US condition separately and release was monitored longitudinally for 14 days post-ADV. Fig. 7 (A) shows the release profile from a bi-layer ARS when only the upper layer, which contained C6-dextran594
, was exposed to US at 3.25 MHz (Pr = 3 MPa) under the +SWF condition. The focus of the transducer was placed at the air interface. Since the axial length of the beam at the focus was ~ 4 mm at 3.25 MHz, locating the focus of the transducer at the air interface ensured ADV throughout the thickness of the top layer without activating the second payload from the bottom layer. Fig. 7 (A) shows up to 80% release from the upper layer by day 14 post-ADV while release from the lower layer was not significantly different than the -US control. A corresponding microscopy image of the bi-layer scaffold when exposed to this US condition is shown in Fig. 7 (B).

Fig. 7 (C) and (D) demonstrate, respectively, the release profile and microscopy image of a bi-layer ARS when only the lower gel, which contained C6-dextran488, was exposed to US at 8.6 MHz (Pr = 7 MPa). Payload release from the bottom layer reached up to 70% by day 14. Payload release from the lower layer would be slower, even without the presence of any gas bubbles, since the diffusion time increases quadratically with distance [58]. Payload release from the upper gel was not significantly different than the control through day 5. Significant release from the upper layer was observed beginning on day 7. This delayed release from the upper gel, although not exposed to US, may be the result of the growth of the ADV bubbles in the lower layer due to in-gassing, resulting in expansion of the lower gel and therefore imparting compressive and tensile stresses on the upper gel. The applied stresses might be larger than the tensile and compressive strengths of the C6 emulsions resulting in release of the payload. In our previous publication we reported a 233% increase in the volume of ARSs containing C5 emulsions three days post-ADV relative to non-exposed ones [29]. Note that since ARSs tend to adhere to the inside of the BioFlex well after polymerization, they would experience different rates of expansion since the middle section of ARSs would expand faster than the portions constrained to the plate walls. Similar to Fig. 4 (C), K was significantly greater for ARSs exposed to Pr = 7 MPa at 8.6 MHz (Fig. 7 (C)) compared to Pr = 3 MPa at 3.25 MHz (Fig. 7 (A)). The potential impact of varying the excitation frequency on the rate constant is currently under study.

Frequency-dependent, sequential release of two dextran payloads from a bi-layer ARS is illustrated in Fig. 8 (A), using similar parameters as in Fig. 7 (A) and (C). The first payload (dextran594) was released on day 0 at 3.25 MHz (Pr = 3 MPa) and the second payload (dextran488) was released on day 4 at 8.6 MHz (Pr = 7 MPa). As seen in Fig. 8 (A), release of the second payload was not significantly different than the control before activation on day 4, showing spatiotemporally-controlled release via ADV. The overall release was 73.0 ± 7.8% and 62.6 ± 8.9% for the first (dextran594) and second (dextran488) payloads on day 14, respectively. Release of the second payload from the lower layer could have been impacted by the standing wave field created by the bubbles which were generated in the upper layer via ADV on day 0. Fig. 8 (B) displays the sequential release of two growth factors relevant to angiogenesis, using similar acoustic parameters as in Fig. 8 (A). The overall bFGF and PDGF released over 14 days was 74.9 ± 7.9% and 54.9 ± 7.8%, respectively.

Higher excitation frequencies enable better spatial selectivity of ADV in ARSs as a result of a tighter focal volume as well as lower probability of IC-related damage to large molecular payloads or adjacent cells at the cost of increased exposure time. Additionally, bubble growth at the same excitation pressure correlates inversely with excitation frequency [59]. A longer rarefactional pressure at low excitation frequency results in a larger maximum bubble diameter. Using the Rayleigh model at an acoustic pressure of 4.5 MPa, Burgess and Porter [60] estimated a maximum radius of 61.7 ± 5.2 µm and 12.4 ± 2.1 µm for vaporized PFP emulsions with an initial diameter of 200 nm at 1 MHz and 5 MHz, respectively. Growth of generated bubbles in the subsequent cycles of a vaporization pulse as well as post-excitation growth depend on parameters such as dissolved gas concentration, ambient pressure, temperature, volume fraction of emulsions, and fibrin concentration. Optical studies have shown that C5 bubbles generated within the first cycles of a multi-cycle vaporization pulse respond to subsequent cycles [61–63]. Although number of cycles has not impacted the ADV threshold in past studies [64,65], it significantly impacts the ultimate fate of the nucleated bubble (i.e., re-condensation, stable oscillation, or IC). In this work, since dual frequency studies were conducted at different amplitudes and number of cycles, no exact comparison can be made on the resultant bubbles generated through ADV. Future studies will focus on the effect of frequency of excitation on the growth of ADV bubbles in the viscoelastic fibrin gels over physiologically relevant time points and their potential impact on the payload release.

US frequency and pressure are two critical parameters associated with ADV. In our previous work on dual-payload release, we used a single excitation frequency to sequentially release two payloads from a single layer ARS containing two emulsions [8]. The first payload was incorporated into an emulsion with a lower boiling point PFC (e.g., C5 or C6) while the second payload was contained in an emulsion with a higher boiling point PFC (e.g., C7). A lower excitation pressure was used to release the first payload, which relied on the direct dependence of the ADV threshold with PFC boiling point. The major limitation of this approach was the presence of generated ADV bubbles following the first payload release, thus shadowing subsequent US exposures.

In the current study, dual-payload release was conducted by integrating the frequency-dependence of the ADV threshold with the effect of a SWF. A bi-layer structure was designed such that the payload in the upper layer was released first at a lower frequency followed by the second payload release from the lower layer at a higher frequency. This approach eliminated the attenuating effects of ADV bubbles on the subsequent exposures and required only one PFC formulation. Note that PFC emulsions have a lower speed of sound (~500 m/s) and higher density compared to water, resulting in a higher acoustic impedance mismatch and surface reflectivity [66,67]. We previously showed that reflection from micron-size C6 emulsions in the ARSs increased linearly with the emulsion volume fraction and frequency [21]. Therefore, for the dual-payload release studies, a lower volume fraction of C6 emulsions was chosen for the higher frequency excitations.

In this work, we first studied the impact of a SWF on payload release from single-payload containing ARSs using ADV. At 2.5 MHz (Pr = 4 MPa), elevated amplitudes due to the constructive superposition in the SWF increased payload release from C6 double emulsions by 21% on day 10 compared to the ARSs in -SWF condition. Fibrin degradation was shown to correlate inversely with the excitation amplitude, being highest for the sham ARSs and lowest for ARSs at 8 MPa in the SWF, likely due to hindered diffusivity within the fibrin network under mechanical strain. We experimentally verified a strategy for generating a SWF for an ARS implanted in situ by creating a pre-formed bubble wall using C5 emulsions. Additionally, bubble growth, generated via ADV, in ARSs was characterized in +SWF and –SWF conditions. Sequential release of two dextran payloads from ARSs, in a bi-layer structure, was studied using temporally staggered ADV at 3.25 MHz (day 0) and 8.6 MHz (day 4). The overall release was 73 ± 7.8% and 62.56 ± 8.9% for the first (dextran594) and second (dextran488) payloads by day 14, respectively. Similarly, for growth factor release, the overall bFGF and PDGF released over 14 days was 74.9 ± 7.9% and 54.9 ± 7.8%, respectively.

Kripfgans: Conceptualization, Software. J. Brian Fowlkes: Conceptualization. Mario L. Fabiilli: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.
